front: Explain the difference between CSS Grid and Flexbox. When would you use each?
back: Grid is for two-dimensional layouts (rows AND columns simultaneously). Flexbox is for one-dimensional layouts (a single row OR column). Use Grid for page layouts, dashboard grids, and complex component arrangements. Use Flexbox for navigation bars, card rows, centering content, and distributing space in one direction. They work well together: Grid for the outer layout, Flexbox for inner component alignment.

front: What are design tokens and why do they matter?
back: Design tokens are the smallest units of a design system: colours, spacing, typography, shadows, border radii stored as platform-agnostic variables. They matter because they create a single source of truth across design and engineering, enable theming (dark mode, brand variants), ensure consistency, and make large-scale changes trivial. Typically structured as primitive tokens (blue-500) mapped to semantic tokens (color-primary) mapped to component tokens (button-bg).

front: How would you implement a compound component pattern in React?
back: Compound components share implicit state via React Context. The parent component provides context, children consume it. Example: Tabs.Root manages active tab state, Tabs.List renders the tab bar, Tabs.Trigger updates the active tab, Tabs.Content shows the active panel. Benefits: flexible composition, clean API, separation of concerns. Use React.createContext, a provider in the root, and useContext in child components.

front: Explain the CSS cascade, specificity, and inheritance.
back: Cascade: the algorithm that resolves conflicts when multiple rules target the same element. Order of priority: importance (!important), origin (author vs user vs browser), specificity, source order. Specificity: inline styles (1000) > IDs (100) > classes/attributes/pseudo-classes (10) > elements/pseudo-elements (1). Inheritance: some properties (color, font-family) inherit from parents, others (margin, padding, border) do not. Use inherit, initial, unset, revert to control inheritance.

front: What is the virtual DOM and how does React's reconciliation work?
back: The virtual DOM is a lightweight JavaScript representation of the actual DOM. When state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM tree, diffs it against the previous one (reconciliation), and applies only the minimal set of real DOM changes (commit phase). Key heuristics: elements of different types produce different trees, developer-provided keys help identify which children have changed. React 18 introduced concurrent rendering which can interrupt reconciliation.

front: How do you ensure accessibility in a component library?
back: Start with semantic HTML (button, nav, main, article). Add ARIA attributes only when HTML semantics are insufficient. Implement keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, Arrow keys). Manage focus: trap focus in modals, return focus on close. Use aria-live regions for dynamic content. Ensure 4.5:1 colour contrast (WCAG AA). Test with screen readers (VoiceOver, NVDA). Add visible focus indicators. Follow WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices for complex widgets like comboboxes, tabs, and dialogs.

front: Explain React Server Components vs Client Components.
back: Server Components render on the server, never ship JavaScript to the client, can directly access databases and file systems, and cannot use state, effects, or browser APIs. Client Components render on the client (with optional SSR), can use useState, useEffect, event handlers, and browser APIs. Use Server Components by default for data fetching and static content. Add 'use client' only when you need interactivity. Server Components can import Client Components but not vice versa.

front: What is CSS containment and how does it improve performance?
back: CSS contain property tells the browser that an element's internals are independent from the rest of the page. contain: layout means the element's internal layout does not affect external elements. contain: paint means nothing inside paints outside the element's bounds. contain: size means the element's size is independent of its children. contain: content is a shorthand for layout + paint. This lets the browser skip recalculating parts of the page it knows have not changed, improving rendering performance.

front: How would you implement a responsive design system that works across breakpoints?
back: Use fluid typography with clamp() for font sizes that scale smoothly. Define breakpoints as design tokens. Use CSS Grid with auto-fit/auto-fill and minmax() for layouts that adapt without media queries where possible. Use container queries for component-level responsiveness instead of viewport-based media queries. Define spacing as relative units (rem) that scale with root font size. Test on real devices, not just browser resize.

front: Explain the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components in React.
back: Controlled: React state is the source of truth. The component receives its current value via props and notifies changes via callbacks (value + onChange). Parent controls the state. Uncontrolled: the DOM is the source of truth. Use refs to read values when needed (defaultValue + ref). Use controlled when you need validation, conditional disabling, or formatting on input. Use uncontrolled for simple forms or when integrating with non-React code. Controlled gives more power but requires more boilerplate.

front: What are the key considerations when building a component library from scratch?
back: 1) Token architecture: primitive, semantic, and component-level tokens. 2) API design: composition over configuration, consistent prop naming. 3) Accessibility: WCAG AA minimum, keyboard nav, screen reader support. 4) Styling strategy: CSS modules, Tailwind, or CSS-in-JS, with theming support. 5) Documentation: Storybook with interactive examples. 6) Testing: unit, integration, visual regression, a11y audits. 7) Versioning: semver, changelogs, migration guides. 8) Tree-shaking: ensure unused components are not bundled.

front: How does the browser rendering pipeline work?
back: Parse HTML to build DOM tree. Parse CSS to build CSSOM. Combine into Render tree (only visible elements). Layout: calculate position and size of each element. Paint: fill in pixels (text, colours, images, borders). Composite: combine painted layers into final image. Reflow (layout recalculation) is expensive: triggered by changing dimensions, adding/removing elements. Repaint is cheaper: triggered by colour changes, visibility. Compositing is cheapest: triggered by transform, opacity changes.

front: Explain the React hooks rules and why they exist.
back: Two rules: 1) Only call hooks at the top level, never inside conditions, loops, or nested functions. 2) Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks. These rules exist because React relies on the ORDER of hook calls to associate state with the correct hook between renders. If you conditionally call a hook, the order changes and React gets confused about which state belongs to which hook. The ESLint plugin eslint-plugin-react-hooks enforces these rules.

front: What is tree shaking and how do you ensure your code is tree-shakeable?
back: Tree shaking is dead code elimination: bundlers (webpack, Rollup, esbuild) remove unused exports from the final bundle. To ensure tree-shakeability: use ES modules (import/export, not require/module.exports). Mark packages as side-effect-free in package.json (sideEffects: false). Avoid side effects at module scope. Export individual functions instead of large objects. Use named exports over default exports. Avoid re-exporting entire modules (barrel files can hurt if not configured properly).

front: How would you implement dark mode in a design system?
back: Use CSS custom properties for all colours, mapping semantic tokens to light/dark values. Toggle via a data attribute on the root element (data-theme='dark') or prefers-color-scheme media query. Store user preference in localStorage, fall back to system preference. Transition colours smoothly with CSS transitions on the custom properties. Consider: images may need different versions or filters, shadows need adjustment, and some colours that work in light mode have poor contrast in dark mode.

front: What is the stacking context in CSS and how does z-index work?
back: A stacking context is a three-dimensional conceptualisation of HTML elements along the z-axis. Created by: root element, positioned elements with z-index, opacity less than 1, transform/filter/perspective properties, and more. z-index only works on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky) and flex/grid children. z-index values are scoped to their stacking context: z-index 999 inside a lower stacking context still appears behind z-index 1 in a higher stacking context.

front: Explain React's useCallback and useMemo. When should you actually use them?
back: useMemo caches a computed value between renders. useCallback caches a function reference. Use useMemo when a calculation is genuinely expensive (filtering/sorting large lists, complex math). Use useCallback when passing callbacks to memoised child components (wrapped in React.memo) to prevent unnecessary re-renders. Do NOT use them everywhere: the overhead of memoisation often exceeds the cost of recalculation. Profile first, optimise second. React Compiler (React 19+) aims to make manual memoisation unnecessary.

front: How do you handle forms in React? Compare approaches.
back: Native HTML forms with FormData: simplest, works without JavaScript, good for progressive enhancement. Controlled components: React state per field, real-time validation, but verbose. React Hook Form: uncontrolled under the hood with refs, minimal re-renders, great DX with register/handleSubmit/watch. Zod + React Hook Form: schema-based validation, type-safe, single source of truth for validation rules. Server Actions (Next.js): form submissions handled server-side, progressive enhancement built in.

front: What are Web Components and how do they compare to React components?
back: Web Components are browser-native: Custom Elements (define new HTML tags), Shadow DOM (encapsulated styles and markup), HTML Templates (inert markup for cloning). They work without a framework and are framework-agnostic. React components are virtual DOM based, use JSX, have richer state management and ecosystem. Trade-offs: Web Components have true style encapsulation but worse DX, limited state management, and no SSR story. React has better tooling, ecosystem, and DX but requires a runtime. They can coexist: React can render Web Components and vice versa.

front: Explain CSS animations vs JavaScript animations. When would you use each?
back: CSS animations: hardware-accelerated for transform and opacity, declarative, simple to implement, better performance for simple transitions. Use for hover effects, loading spinners, page transitions. JavaScript animations: imperative control, can respond to dynamic values, can be paused/reversed/chained programmatically. Use for complex sequences, physics-based motion, scroll-driven animations, or when you need to coordinate with state. Libraries: Framer Motion (React), GSAP (framework-agnostic). Web Animations API bridges both worlds.
